summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where there are final orders in place for the children to live with the mother and spend time with the father – Where the father has failed to comply with the final orders to return the children – Where an application was made by the father to reopen the parenting proceedings a week after final orders were made pursuant to s 65DAAA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) – Where the mother seeks to enforce the final orders including a recovery order for the children – Where the father acknowledged and the Court finds there has been no change in circumstances – Whether the father asserts the final orders are ‘unworkable’ – Children ordered to be returned to the mother via the Court Children’s Service – Recovery order made – Not in the children’s best interests for there to be further litigation – No variation to final orders. FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Harmful proceedings –Where the Court is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe that the children and/or the mother would suffer harm if the father instituted further proceedings – Order made pursuant to s 102QAC of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) prohibiting the father from instituting proceedings under the Act.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

KARI J:

INTRODUCTION

These are interlocutory parenting proceedings relating to the parties two daughters now aged thirteen and eleven.
The current applications before the court arise against a backdrop of final orders having been made by Jarrett J on 7 August 2025 in circumstances where he heard a trial in these proceedings in January 2025.
The final orders made by the court provided for a change of the primary living arrangements of the children from the father to the mother.
To date that change of living arrangements has not been implemented.
The father now asks the court to reconsider the parenting arrangements for these children. He did so when he commenced the current tranche of litigation on 14 August 2025, exactly one week following the delivery of judgment and the making of final orders.
The children have the benefit of having their best interests advanced by an Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”), who is the same ICL that was earlier assigned to the children in the previous tranche of proceedings.
Both the mother and the ICL oppose the orders sought by the father, and instead the mother promotes, and the ICL supports, orders being made to ensure that the final orders made by the court on 7 August 2025 providing for the children to live with the mother are implemented; in particular the mother asks the court to make a recovery order. They each otherwise do not consider that there should be any further litigation regarding the parenting arrangements for these children.
In addition to these issues, at a hearing on 30 January 2026 the court also indicated to the parties that the court was giving consideration as to whether a “harmful proceedings order” within the meaning of s 102QAC of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) ought be made in relation to the father, and directed the parties to address the court about this topic at the scheduled hearing of the extant interlocutory applications.

BACKGROUND

When regard is had to the orders of Jarrett J and the reasons published 7 August 2025 (Tyler & Cadmon [2025] FedCFamC1F 389 (“the trial reasons”), the short relevant background is as follows:
(a) The parties to these proceedings are the children’s parents.

(b) The parties commenced their relationship in May 2010, began living together in January 2011 and separated in June 2016 (at [21]).

(c) The child X was born in 2012. X was diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder in June 2023 and she has the benefit of an NDIS plan (at [76]). She presents with emotional dysregulation and anxiety (at [121]).

(d) The child Y was born in 2015.

(e) The father has re-partnered with a British national, they are engaged to be married and they have a young child together named D (at [17]).

In relation to the parenting arrangements for the children, and again with regard to the trial reasons:
(a) Prior to separation the mother was the primary carer for the children (at [22]).

(b) Following the parties’ separation in June 2016 the children lived in a week about arrangement with changeovers occurring on Sundays (at [23]). Jarrett J found that the mother agreed to these arrangements as a result of “her perception about how the [father] would treat her if he did not get his own way” (at [30]).

(c) These arrangements broke down on 30 August 2021 as a result of a physical altercation that resulted in the mother’s partner assaulting a friend of the father during an incident which also involved both parents (at [33]-[37]). Whilst the children were not present during this incident (at [38]), the children were subsequently retained by the father. The mother was ultimately able to recover the child Y from school, with X remaining in the father’s care (at [39]).

(d) This episode in August 2021 led to the father commencing parenting proceedings on 10 September 2021 (at [40]), and ultimately a resumption of the children’s week about living arrangement.

(e) The week about living arrangement again broke down some time in 2022 such that the children were living with the father and spending alternate weekends with the mother (at [41]). Findings were made that the father had been “manipulative” in engineering this outcome (at [46]).

(f) On 20 July 2022 the parties entered into final consent orders which provided for (at [48]):

(i) The parties to have equal shared parental responsibility.

(ii) The children to live with the parties in a week about living arrangement until 1 March 2023 and thereafter that they live predominately with the father and spend alternate weekend time with the mother from Friday afternoon until Monday morning, together with a period of time spending after school until 7.00pm each Wednesday.

(g) The July 2022 final orders however were not implemented because on the father’s case the children “refused to transition into the mother’s care and the mother never sought to implement the orders” (at [49]). Findings were made that the father “exerted considerable pressure” upon the mother at this time (at [50]) and that the mother had entered into orders that provided for the children to live with her on a shared care basis until March 2023 so that she could continue to receive Centrelink benefits (at [52]).

(h) From at least July 2022 until January 2023 the children lived with the father and spent regular alternate weekends and each intervening Wednesday with the mother (at 56]). Thereafter however the children’s time spending with the mother became fractured and more sporadic (at [55] and [62]).

(i) Proceedings were recommenced by the father in February 2023.

(j) On 28 September 2023 orders were made suspending the mother’s time with the children pursuant to the final orders of 20 July 2022, and replaced with an order that the children’s time with the mother be “subject to their wishes” each alternate weekend from 3.30pm Friday until 7.00pm Sunday and each intervening Wednesday from 3.30pm until 7.00pm (at [81]). These arrangements were put in place alongside family therapy, which ultimately was of no success as both children “openly voiced their refusal to spend time” with the mother, and the father facilitated the children’s refusals (at [84]-[92]).

(k) In October 2024 the father attempted to obtain a “restraining order” against the mother “for [X]” in circumstances where the mother had attended the children’s school [.... Findings were made that criticised the father’s conduct in this regard (at [93]).

(l) By January 2025 at the time of trial, the children had not spent time with the mother pursuant to the orders of 28 September 2023 or otherwise (at [92]).

At the commencement of the trial in January 2025:
(a) The father was seeking orders that provided for the children to live with him, that he have sole parental responsibility, that he be permitted to relocate the children to live with him, his new partner and the children’s half-sibling in the United Kingdom (at [3]).

(b) The mother sought orders that, subject to certain relatively minor variations, the final consent orders made on 20 July 2022 continue in operation (at [8]).

The trial reasons reveal that the parties agreed that the mother’s time spending with the children was impacted because the children began to disengage and/or refuse to spend time with her. Whilst it appeared to be the father’s case that he had done what he could to ensure the children spent time with the mother, findings were made rejecting the father’s assertions and preferring the mother’s version of events. Importantly, findings were made which were highly critical of the father’s role in the breakdown of the children’s relationship with the mother (for example at [60], [64], [65], [66], [69]).
In this context, the evidence at trial was that the father had unilaterally taken the child X to consult with a psychologist in mid 2023 (at [72]). Findings were made criticising the father’s conduct in doing so (at [74]).
Of some significance the trial reasons record:
Ultimately, the [father’s] evidence about the failure of the children to spend time with the [mother] was simple. He says that the children had expressed a very clear view that they did not wish to spend time with the [mother] and he would not force them to do so. He accepted that aside from asking them if they wanted to spend time with the [mother], he had not done anything to deliver the children to the [mother], nor in any real way encourage them to do so. Thus, it was the [father’s] position that unless either child expressed a clear wish to communicate or spend time with the [mother], it would not happen.
In relation to that evidence of the father, Jarrett J was highly critical, with the trial reasons recording the following:
It is simply insufficient for the [father] to contend that he did not insist upon the children spending time with the [mother] and thereby maintain their relationship with her because the children simply did not want to. Children are entitled to a relationship with each of their parents and each of their parents has an obligation to ensure that those entitlements are met. It is not generally a sufficient discharge of that obligation for a parent to simply say that, “I was following my child’s wishes”. To adopt such a position, in the great majority of cases, is to simply fail one’s children. But what has occurred here is more than that. For example, the statements made by the children to [the family therapist] demonstrate that the [father] has denigrated the [mother] to the children and has told them falsehoods no doubt for the purpose of souring their relationship with her and ultimately leading to their rejection of her. Perhaps the best example of this is the children’s statements to [family therapist] that the [father] had told them that the [mother] had said that she hoped that their half sibling (the child of the [father] and [his partner]) did not survive her surgery. It was never put to the [mother] in cross-examination that she had said those things. I am satisfied that the [father] said those things to the children but I am equally satisfied that it is more than likely that the [mother] had never said such a thing to the children.
The [father’s] failure to foster, encourage and facilitate the relationship between the children and the [mother] has had significant consequences for these children. As Ms [H] was given to observe in her report at [178], “Furthermore, one’s relationship with a parent is one of the most profound in informing psycho-emotional development and identity formation.” By reason of the [father’s] actions the children have been denied their relationship with the [mother].
Court Child Expert Ms H prepared a report in the earlier proceedings. The trial reasons record:
... Ms [H] thought that should the [father] moved to the United Kingdom without the children, leaving the children to live with the [mother], there would be concern for the children’s psycho-emotional well-being and development. She thought that the children would suffer a deep sense of loss and grief considering what Ms [H] described as their significant attachment to the [father]. In cross examination she added that the children would experience a sense of rejection and abandonment. She thought that would take a significant amount of support from the [mother] as well as the therapist, for them to be able to make sense of that and to not internalise it as a rejection of something that they had done wrong. She thought that if the children were separated from the [father’s] primary care to live with the [mother], they would likely experience psychological distress potentially leading to regression in their development or placing them at risk of developing mental health issues such as depression and anxiety or adjustment disorder. I accept Ms [H’s] evidence about these matters.
It was also Ms [H’s] firm opinion, both in her written and oral testimony, that the children’s residence should remain in Australia irrespective of where the [father] chose to live. There are a number of matters that inform Ms [H’s] opinion about that. Among them and the first nominated by her was the potential for the children to completely lose their relationship with the [mother] or the “hope of a relationship” with the [mother]. She also thought that there were a number of unknowns for the children that would impact their emotional, psychological well-being, education and development.
The trial reasons also record findings that:
(a) The children were not at risk of physical harm from either parent (at [102]);

(b) Both parents have in different ways caused emotional or psychological harm to the children (at [103]), with that caused by the mother paling in comparison to that caused by the father (at [106]);

(c) The children were at “significant risk of emotional or psychological harm” if they were to relocate with the father to the United Kingdom, because the court had no confidence that that the father would “promote” the children’s relationship with the mother and ensure that they spend time with her (at [111]);

(d) The father did not want to make the children’s relationship with the mother “work” (at [111]);

(e) The children would experience emotional or psychological harm if they were to remain living in Australia with the mother and if the father were to relocate to the United Kingdom, and that they would need “considerable support and assistance” (at [112]);

(f) On balance the mother’s proposal would “promote the emotional and psychological safety” of the children “more than will” the father’s proposal (at [113]);

(g) The father does not have an “appropriate capacity” to meet the children’s emotional or psychological needs “and in particular their psychological need for an appropriate and enduring relationship” with the mother (at [127]);

(h) The father and his partner have a “lack of respect” for the mother “as the children’s other parent” (at [128]);

(i) The mother has the “capacity to meet the children’s day-to-day physical and intellectual needs” (at [129]);

(j) The mother has the capacity to meet the children’s medical needs (at [131]);

(k) The mother has the capacity to meet the children’s emotional and psychological needs and that she will promote the children’s relationship with the father (at [132]);

(l) The father has “demonstrated an unwillingness to ensure that the children have a relationship” with the mother (at [143]);

(m) The father was a person “who is given to making threats. And not just insignificant threats, but rather threats to kill people and to make sure that the [mother] does not see the children ever again.” (at [29]); and

(n) The father “demonstrated himself to have dogmatic and unchangeable views” (at [29]).

Of some significance, during the course of the trial an order was made by the court for the children to spend time with the mother, and they did so (at [111]). In that regard the trial reasons record:
...That episode demonstrates, in my view that the [father] does have the wherewithal to ensure these children to spend time with the [mother], but that it is just that he is unwilling to ensure that it happens because it does not suit his purposes.
In addition, and again during the trial, orders were made which put into effect, on an interim basis and pending delivery of judgment, a time spending arrangement between the mother and the children in accordance with the father’s proposal pending any relocation of he and the children.
The orders made on 23 January 2025 provided for:
(a) The children to spend time with the mother for a period of 8 weeks, from 10.00am until 4.00pm each Saturday as and from 25 January 2025 (incorrectly recorded as February in the order);

(b) The father to facilitate Skype calls between the children and the mother each Sunday between 8.30 and 9.30pm and each Wednesday between 6.30am and 7.00am, together with such other times as requested by the children; and

(c) The parties to engage in further family therapy and reunification counselling.

EVENTS FOLLOWING THE CONCLUSION OF THE TRIAL AND DELIVERY OF JUDGMENT

A period of approximately seven months elapsed between the conclusion of the trial and the delivery of final judgment on 7 August 2025.
During that time it appears uncontroversial from the evidence now before the court that the children did spend some time with the mother as contemplated by the orders made 23 January 2025.
On 4 June 2025 the mother filed an Application in a Proceeding in which she sought orders for time spending with the children. It does not appear that this application was determined prior to the making of the final orders on 7 August 2025. Indeed, it appears that the same was not heard given the imminent publication of the judgment.
In her affidavit filed 29 August 2025 the mother set out with some detail that which had transpired following the orders for her to spend time with the children on 23 January 2025. It is not necessary to set out each and every occasion of time spending in detail in these reasons. That said, it is important to record the mother’s evidence as follows:
(a) The first occasion of time spending took place on 25 January 2025. Both children were able to transition to the mother (paragraph 8).

(b) Time spending with both children took place with the children transitioning to the mother on each 1 February 2025, 8 February 2025, 15 February 2025 and 22 February 2025 (paragraph 10).

(c) The mother asserts that the father refused to handover the children to her on 1 March 2025 unless she agreed to permit him to relocate the children to the United Kingdom and that he was planning to move with them in the next month. Whilst a little unclear it appears that X did not transition to the mother for time spending but that Y did (paragraph 11).

(d) On 8 March 2025 the children spent time with the mother and had a sleepover at the mother’s house with the father’s consent (paragraph 14).

(e) Y spent overnight time with the mother (paragraphs 15 and 16):

(i) On each 15 and 22 March 2025;

(ii) For two consecutive overnights from 28 March 2025; and

(iii) For a week from 5 April 2025.

(f) On 22 April 2025 the father and his partner met with the mother and proposed that he take X to live with him in the UK and that Y remain in Australia and live with the mother (paragraph 17).

(g) The father ceased facilitating the children’s time spending with the mother whatsoever from 30 April 2025, other than to facilitate time spending between the mother and Y on Mother’s Day (paragraph 20).

(h) The father told the mother that seeing her was “causing Y massive anxiety and she had developed an eating disorder” (paragraph 21).

THE FINAL ORDERS MADE 7 AUGUST 2025

The final orders made 7 August 2025 were as follows:
The applicant’s Amended Application for Final Orders filed 16 November 2024 is dismissed.
2. All parenting orders are discharged.
The [mother] have sole decision making responsibility for all major long-term issues for the children [X], born [...] 2012, and [Y], born [...] 2015.
The [mother] inform the [father] on any decisions pertaining to major long-term issues affecting the children.
Commencing no later than 10.00am on Saturday 9 August 2025, [X] and [Y] live with the [mother].

6. [X] and [Y] spend time with [father]:
(a) in Australia on the Australian long school holidays commencing from 1 January in 2026 in the 2025/2026 school holidays and in each alternate year thereafter; and

(b) commencing from the conclusion of the school term in the 2026/2027 school holidays and in each alternate year thereafter with time to occur in the United Kingdom or Australia at the option of the [father].

Notwithstanding order 6(b), [X] and [Y] shall not spend time with the [father] in the United Kingdom until such time as he has provided evidence, to the [mother’s] satisfaction, that he has caused the registration of these orders in, or the making of mirror orders by, a court of competent jurisdiction in the United Kingdom.
Handovers shall occur at Adelaide Airport at the commencement and conclusion of the children’s time with the applicant.
The [father] shall bear the costs of his own and all the children’s travel expenses including airfares, passports and accommodation.
The [mother] shall facilitate Skype calls between the children and the [father] as follows:
(a) each Sunday between 8.30pm and 9.30pm Adelaide time (ACST);

(b) each alternate Wednesday between 6.30am and 7.00am ACST;

(c) at such other times as requested by the children.
The [mother] shall continue to engage with the family therapy and reunification counselling services for herself and the children provided by [Ms B].
The [mother] shall continue to attend appointments with [Ms B] on dates and times nominated by [Ms B] and follow all her recommendations.
The [mother] shall cease attending [Ms B], once [Ms B] deems that the provision of her services in relation to the children and the [mother] is no longer required.
The [father] shall forthwith take all steps necessary to transfer the child [X’s] NDIS plan into the [mother’s] name prior to his departure to the United Kingdom.
Each party shall keep the other informed of their residential address, contact telephone number and email address and advise the other party of any change within twenty four (24) hours.
The parties shall use the AppClose parenting app (or such other app as may be agreed from time to time) for the purpose of communication about the children’s health, education and general development, with all such communication to be polite and respectful.
The [mother] must keep the [father] advised as to the names, addresses and telephone numbers of the children’s treating health professionals, and shall keep the [father] informed of any such changes in those details.
In the event of a medical emergency, the party who the children are with is to contact the other party by way of telephone as soon as reasonably practicable of the emergency occurring, and provide full details of the emergency.
The [mother] shall keep the [father] informed in relation to the home address of the children, the party’s telephone contact number and email address and in the event that any of those contact details change, the respondent must notify the other within forty eight (48) hours of such a change including by providing the updated contact details.
The parties are restrained, and an injunction hereby issues restraining each of them from:
(a) abusing, denigrating or criticising the other parent or any member of his or her household in the presence or hearing of the children, or allowing anyone else to do so; or

(b) allowing the children to be exposed to any violent or aggressive behaviour between or involving the parties.

The independent children’s lawyer shall at a time that is a soon as is conveniently possible, meet with [X] and [Y] for the purposes of explaining these orders to them and for that purpose both the [father] and the [mother] shall do all things necessary to ensure the children’s attendance with the independent children’s lawyer for that purpose.
Otherwise, all other applications, including any outstanding Application in a Proceeding, are dismissed.

EVENTS SINCE THE MAKING OF THE FINAL ORDERS ON 7 AUGUST 2025

In his affidavit filed 14 August 2025, the father’s evidence is that following the delivery of judgment:
(a) On 9 August 2025, he attended the R Police Station at 10.00am to effect handover of the children (paragraph 8). The children however became “acutely distressed and refused to leave with the mother” (paragraph 9). Officers from South Australia Police attended and advised the father to remove the children “for their immediate welfare” (paragraph 10).

(b) On 12 August 2025:

(i) He received a letter from “our long term family General Practitioner” regarding X’s presentation and recent events (paragraph 13).

(ii) He received an email from Y’s psychologist to “pause her sessions pending access to relevant portions of Final Orders” (paragraph 14).

(c) The Department for Child Protection (“DCP”) suggested that the “children prepare handwritten letters setting out their views and wishes” (paragraph 15).

Annexed to the father’s affidavit filed 14 August 2025 are various documents including:
(a) A letter from GP Dr U dated 12 August 2025;

(b) Communications between the father and a DCP worker dated 10 August 2025;

(c) Communications between the father and the mother’s solicitor and the ICL between 8 August 2025 and 11 August 2025; and

(d) Handwritten letters which the father asserts were written by each of the children dated 10 August 2025.

From those documents it appears that:
(a) On Friday 8 August 2025, the day after judgment was delivered and the final orders were made:
(i) At 11.06am, the mother’s solicitor wrote to the father by email confirming that pursuant to the final orders the children were to transition to live with the mother as and from 10.00am on Saturday 9 August 2025. A proposal was made that the children be collected from the father’s home by an “extended family member” of the mother and then delivered to the mother’s residence. A request was made that the father “support the children to transition to our client’s care as ordered”.

(ii) At 11.10am the father responded and indicated that he had advised the children of the orders and that both children “are currently experiencing significant distress and resistance, particularly [X]”. The father added that he had made contact with the ICL to speak with the children and “assist with the transition and ensure their welfare is protected”. The father added that he was not comfortable with the mother’s proposed handover arrangements and instead suggested that the children remain with him for the weekend, that he deliver them to school on Monday and that the mother collect the children from school without him present.

(iii) At 11.55am the ICL communicated with the parties to advise that she did not support the father’s proposed handover arrangements, and emphasised that the father’s proposal did not comply with the requirement in the final order that the children transition to the mother “no later than 10am on 9 August” (Emphasis in the original). The ICL suggested that the father take the children to the mother’s home, or the mother collect them from the father’s home, or in the alternative that handover take place at a neutral location “such as McDonalds”. The ICL then indicated that she would meet with the children “as soon as possible after they have transitioned to the mother”.

(iv) At 12.04pm the father emailed the mother’s solicitor and the ICL indicating that he would comply with the final orders and proposed that handover take place either at McDonalds or a police station. The father indicated that “given the children’s current distress, I believe it is in their best interests to avoid a home attendance by the mother or her family, as this may heighten their anxiety”.

(v) At 1.37pm the mother’s solicitor wrote to the father and the ICL and indicated that the mother considered the children should be afforded “privacy” and that she wanted to avoid them becoming distressed in a public venue such as McDonalds. The mother indicated that she did not support either McDonalds or a police station as she considered the latter would likely heighten the children’s anxiety. The mother proposed that the father deliver the children to the home of the maternal grandmother.

(vi) At 1.48pm the father responded, “I need to be very clear” and went on to identify that “this transition is already an extremely emotional and distressing moment” for the children “and me”. The father proposed two alternate locations at either Westfield Suburb S or Bunnings Suburb S. The father stipulated that “failing agreement to one of the above locations, I will attend R Police Station at 10am for the handover” (Emphasis in the original).

(vii) At 2.51pm the mother’s solicitor responded with the mother reluctantly agreeing to the father’s proposed handover at the police station, making the point that it was not the mother’s preferred option and that she did not agree to the father’s proposals including “a busy shopping centre entrance”. The mother requested that the father “immediately leave the handover location to assist the children’s transition”.

(viii) The father responded at 2.57pm agreeing to the handover at the police station and agreeing to leave immediately following handover.

(b) On 10 August 2025:

(i) At 10.18am a Senior Social Worker from the Department for Child Protection (“DCP”), Ms Q sent an email to the father. Implicit in that email is that Ms Q had earlier spoken with the father (which from a reading of the response from the DCP dated 9 December 2025 to a section 67ZBD order made by the court suggests there was a telephone call that took place on 8 August 2025). That email set out the worker’s understanding that “the children may wish to speak to the Department to express their views regarding feeling unsafe in the mother’s care” and suggested that she was “happy for the children to write a letter expressing their views/wishes and for you to email this to me and I can attach as part of the intake”. The worker otherwise made clear to the father that they were not giving the father legal advice, and that the DCP did not “necessarily get involved in Family Law Court matters, unless DCP assess that the children are at significant risk of harm or unsafe”.

(ii) The father responded to this email from Ms Q at 1.26pm setting out his understanding of their earlier phone call, which included that he considered she had “invited the children to provide handwritten letters”, and that she had told him “in the Department’s view, no authority (including SAPOL or AFP) should forcibly remove the children from my care against their wishes, given their ages and stated concerns”.

(iii) Ms Q responded to this email at 3.10pm. The response included that the DCP assessed the children as being safe in the father’s care and that no action was required from the DCP.

(iv) A further email was sent by Ms Q at 5.19pm in which she sought to clarify “something that came up in our correspondence”, and in particular the father’s assertion that she had told him that “no authority should enable forcible removal of children from one parent to another”. Ms Q was at pains in that communication to make clear to the father that there were court orders in place for the children and that the DCP “cannot support disregarding Family Court authorities”, that the father should seek legal advice and that any issues needed to be “resolved in the Family Court arena”.

(v) At 8.36pm the father sent an email to the ICL indicating that he had “lodged an urgent Application in a Proceeding (stay pending appeal)”.

(vi) At 1.46pm the ICL responded to the father and enquired whether the father’s communications to her had also been sent to the mother’s solicitor. The ICL also enquired whether the children had attended school that day, and confirmed that she was making arrangements to meet with the children to explain the orders to them.

(c) The father made an application for a grant of legal aid on 11 August 2025.

(d) Also on 11 August 2025:

(i) At 2.07pm the father sent a long email to the ICL addressing a number of topics, including:
the father’s confirmation that the mother’s solicitor had been copied into all relevant correspondence;
the father’s confirmation that handover did not occur on 9 August 2025 due to the “children’s acute distress”;
the father’s advice that the children had not attended school on 11 August 2025 “due to their distress following Saturday’s events and to accommodate urgent health appointments booked for tomorrow” which included a psychologist appointment for Y and a GP and psychologist appointment for X;
the father’s question of the ICL as to whether she still intended to meet with the children to explain the orders “in light of the new information (including SAPOL intervention on August 9, 2025, the children’s handwritten letters, and the medical bookings”;
the father’s advice which read “For your awareness only: my Federal MP’s office has advised that they have raised the safety concerns with the South Australian Attorney-General’s Department (August 11 2025). This is noted purely for transparency and is not intended to influence your independent role”.

(ii) At 2.18pm the mother’s solicitor sent an email to the ICL confirming that handover of the children had not been effected and advising that “the father would not agree to the mother’s handover arrangements and failed to leave the vicinity of the handover location as agreed”.

(iii) At 2.26pm the father responded to the email from the mother’s solicitor to the ICL. The response included the father’s assertions that handover did not occur due to the “children’s acute distress”, that SAPOL had intervened and advised him that he was not in “contravention at that time” and that they “instructed” him not to leave the vicinity. The father denied allegations that he had “manipulated or traumatised the children”.

(e) On 12 August 2025:

(i) Y’s psychologist sent the parties an email requesting information regarding the final orders and otherwise indicating that her sessions would be “paused” until receipt of the new court orders.

(ii) The father obtained a report from GP, Dr U following an appointment which had taken place with X in the father’s presence that day. It is necessary to set out the entire report:

To Whom It May Concern,

Re: [X] (DOB: [...]/2012) – Welfare and Custody Concerns

I am writing this report in my professional capacity following a consultation with [X], who attended my rooms today accompanied by her father, Mr [Tyler]. During the consultation, [X] articulated her thoughts, wishes, and concerns in a coherent and emotionally expressive manner. Her father was present but did not direct or interfere with her communication.

Summary of Concerns

[X] expressed extreme distress regarding the current court order which grants custody of herself and her sister to their mother. She reported a recent incident in which she was taken to a police station for the purpose of facilitating a handover to her mother. [X] became visibly upset, cried uncontrollably, and physically resisted the transition and ultimately locking herself in the car. According to [X], this event was witnessed by attending police officers, who returned the girls to the father’s care. A subsequent attempt to comply with the court order is anticipated to take place this coming weekend.

[X] was adamant and unambiguous in her statement: she does not wish to see or be placed in the care of her mother, now or in the future.
Disclosure of Alleged Abuse
[X] described several incidents that, if verified, would meet the threshold for emotional, physical and psychological abuse. These include:
Being physically hit by both her mother and her mother’s partner.
Being locked outside the home for certain periods of time.
Having all her personal belongings removed, and being confined to a room containing only a bed and clothing.
Being forced to eat foods she strongly dislikes, with one incident involving her maternal grandmother allegedly forcing her face into a bowl of spinach.
Being prevented from participating in a school dress-up event, including not being allowed to dress up.

[X] has a formal diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder. Her emotional and psychological responses including nail-biting, withdrawal, and heightened anxiety have notably intensified since learning of the court order granting custody to her mother.

Observations of the Father’s Home Environment:

I have personally known [Mr Tyler] and his family for over two decades. In both professional and personal contexts, I have consistently observed [Mr Tyler], his partner, and his parents to provide a stable, nurturing, and child-centred environment for [X] and her sister. The care they offer is aligned with the children’s emotional and developmental needs, and I have no reason to question their intentions or parenting capacity.

Clinical Recommendations:

Based on the information disclosed and [X’s] current emotional state, it is my strong clinical opinion that any attempt to return [X] or her sister to her mother’s care or to force contact poses a significant risk of emotional trauma and potential physical harm. The behaviours described by [X] are consistent with patterns of child abuse, and her reactions are aligned with a child experiencing psychological distress and fear.

At this stage, it is not in [X’s] or her sisters best interest to have contact with her mother or her mother’s partner. The risk of harm both physical and psychological is considerable. I strongly recommend that [X] and her sister remain in the care of their father, where they appear safe, supported, and emotionally stable, until further investigations and legal reviews are completed.

If further information or clarification is required, I am available for contact and willing to provide additional insight as necessary.

(Emphasis in the original)

The legal framework

The applicable parenting provisions are those set out in Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).
Section 60B sets out the objects underpinning the parenting provisions in the Act as ones that “ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety; and to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child done at New York on 20 November 1989”.
What constitutes a parenting order is defined in s 64B of the Act and includes relevantly for present purposes the arrangements for the children contained in the final orders made on 7 August 2025.
The Act was amended, with provisions coming into force on 6 May 2024. Relevantly, those new provisions included a codification of, what prior to that date, had been known as the principles and/or rule in Rice & Asplund ((1979) FLC 90-725 (“Rice and Asplund”)). Those amendments are contained in s 65DAAA of the Act and they prescribe the considerations and pathway for the court when it is called upon to reconsider final parenting orders.
The rule in Rice and Asplund became known as that expressed by Evatt CJ (with whom Pawley SJ and Fogarty J agreed) (at 78,905) as follows:
[The court] should not lightly entertain an application to reverse an earlier custody order. To do so would be to invite endless litigation for change is an ever-present factor in human affairs. Therefore, the court would need to be satisfied by the applicant that ... there is some changed circumstance which will justify such a serious step, some new factor arising or, at any rate, some factor which was not disclosed at the previous hearing which would have been material...

(Emphasis added)
Section 65DAAA of the Act provides:
Reconsideration of final parenting orders
(1) If a final parenting order is in force in relation to a child, a court must not reconsider the final parenting order unless:

(a) the court has considered whether there has been a significant change of circumstances since the final parenting order was made; and

(b) the court is satisfied that, in all the circumstances (and taking into account whether there has been a significant change of circumstances since the final parenting order was made), it is in the best interests of the child for the final parenting order to be reconsidered.

(2) For the purposes of determining whether the court is satisfied as mentioned in paragraph (1)(b), and without limiting section 60CC, the court may have regard to any matters that the court considers relevant, including the following:

(a) the reasons for the final parenting order and the material on which it was based;

(b) whether there is any material available that was not available to the court that made the final parenting order;

(c) the likelihood that, if the final parenting order is reconsidered, the court will make a new parenting order that affects the operation of the final parenting order in a significant way (whether by varying, discharging or suspending the final parenting order, in whole or in part, or in some other way);

(d) any potential benefit, or detriment, to the child that might result from reconsidering the final parenting order.

(3) Despite subsection (1), the court may reconsider a final parenting order with the agreement or consent of all the parties to that order.

(4) The failure of a court to comply with subsection (1) does not affect the validity of any order made by the court.

The Full Court in Radecki & Radecki [2024] FedCFamC1A 246; (2024) FLC 94-232 definitively considered the new s 65DAAA provisions, and relevantly confirmed:
(a) That an order dismissing an application for parenting orders (as sought by the mother here) is not itself a parenting order (at [24]); and

(b) That s 65DAAA(1) requires the court to (at [79]):

... contemplate the evidence and to make findings of fact as to what changes in circumstances (if any) there have been since the making of the anterior parenting orders. If there is no positive finding of changed circumstances, that is the end of the matter. If there is a positive finding as to changed circumstances, the second stage of the process requires the Court to make its determination, subject to the overarching best interests principle, as prescribed by s 65DAAA(1)(b) and otherwise having regard to relevant s 60CC considerations and the matters referred to in s 65DAAA(2).

Discussion

Addressing the question of whether there has been a change of circumstance since the making of the final orders on 7 August 2025 is not a difficult process in these proceedings.
This is because during the hearing of the interim applications on 13 February 2026, the father acknowledged, when the question was directly put to him, that there had been no change of circumstances and instead asserted that he considered the orders were “unworkable”. This admission by the father is fatal to his application to have the court revisit the final parenting orders.
More however needs to be said about why the court is satisfied, regardless of this admission by the father, that there has not been a change of circumstances.
When regard is had to the trial reasons, as earlier discussed, the omnipresent theme was the father’s position that the children would not spend time with the mother and that he could not and would not force them to do so. This is a position about which Jarrett J was rightly critical of the father and which weighed heavily in the reasoning resulting in the orders that there be a change of the children’s primary care from the father to the mother.
Indeed, the trial reasons make clear that the court apprehended that there would be difficulty in the children transitioning to live with the mother, particularly if the father were to relocate to the United Kingdom, but that the court had some confidence (relying on the opinion expressed by Ms Q) that the mother would take up appropriate supports to ensure that ultimately the children could be emotionally and psychologically safe in her primary care (at [112]).
Explicit in the father’s position now before the court is that the children will not transition to the mother, either to spend time with her or to live with her as contemplated by the orders of 7 August 2025. That however is hardly a new circumstance, given the position advanced by the father at trial and the events that have shaped these children’s lives since their parents separation discussed throughout the trial reasons and as summarised herein; albeit their more recent refusals have occurred seemingly in the lead up to the making of the final orders and thereafter.
Moreover, and what is deeply troubling to the court, is the court’s apprehension that the father both whilst judgment was reserved (between January and August 2025) and since the making of the final orders has gone out of his way to engineer the very situation that has now arisen with the children’s renewed purported refusal to spend time with the mother. In that regard:
(a) It is difficult not to draw some nefarious coincidence between both children’s willingness to spend time with the mother across February 2025 and that willingness waning in March 2025 before suddenly coming to a halt on 30 April 2025, on the mother’s case (which the father has not specifically responded to), and the father pressuring the mother throughout this period of time to permit his relocation of the children to the United Kingdom; troublingly including a proposal that the siblings be separated.

(b) The flurry of activity the father engaged in within hours of the publication of the trial reasons and orders on 7 August 2025 suggests that the father’s words in supporting the transition of the children to the mother (both in his most recent material filed and in the communications he sent the mother and the ICL), were hollow as they were not matched by his actions, and in particular:

(i) Almost immediately after judgment was delivered the father embarked on what appears to be a frenzy of criticisms which included criticisms of the trial judge allegedly made to a Member of Parliament, the Attorney General of South Australia and the court;

(ii) The lightning speed at which the father obtained a report from a General Practitioner (who incidentally was not a witness relied upon by the father at the trial in January 2025);

(iii) The father’s involvement of the DCP, his apparent miscasting of the information conveyed to him by the DCP and his engineering of letters written by the children; and

(iv) It is not lost on the court that the father in his evidence is entirely silent as to what he told the children about the final orders that had been made, nor how that was conveyed and how he behaved during that interaction, particularly where the final orders included orders for the ICL to speak with the children and explain the final orders to them.

The father’s inveiglement of various professionals in the immediate aftermath of the final orders also requires some further comment, particularly so far as it relates to the report of Dr U. This is because little weight can be attached by the court to the opinions proffered by Dr U in circumstances where amongst other things:
(a) In his own words, Dr U is not independent as he has known the father and his family both professionally and personally for over two decades; and

(b) Dr U has not had the benefit of all of the evidence before the court in these proceedings, and which formed the basis of the final orders made 7 August 2025, together with that before the court on the within applications.

Moreover, the father’s communications to the ICL and the mother’s solicitor regarding handover arrangements, particularly those across 8 and 9 August 2025, almost certainly were a window through which the ultimate failure of the handover arrangements could have been predicted; particularly given their similarity in tone and effect to the examples of communication between the parents referred to in the trial reasons of which Jarrett J was highly critical of the father and his manipulation of the mother.
All of these events, should be considered in the context of the findings in the trial reasons that the father has the capacity to and has been manipulative (at [29] and [46]), has dogmatic and unchangeable views (at [29]), and has little insight into the emotional harm that the children have already endured (at [146]).
During the hearing, the father submitted somewhat tellingly “it [has] become a very messy and complex situation”. This is likely the most insightful submission that could have been made by the father. Sadly however, the court is satisfied that the messiness and complexity of the situation now, as it was when Jarrett J heard the proceedings, is one entirely of the father’s making and within his direct influence to have changed. The father’s ongoing lack of insight as to the impact of his own behaviour on the children, and the likely ongoing emotional and psychological harm he is causing the children is deeply troubling to the court.
For all of these reasons, the court is satisfied that the children’s current refusal to transition to the mother is not some new or changed circumstance. Rather it is to use the colloquial, “more of the same”.
As the question posed by s 65DAAA(1) has been answered in the negative, the court’s enquiry ends. There is to be no wholesale reopening of the parenting arrangements for X and Y and the orders made 7 August 2025 remain in full force and effect. The Initiating Application of the father filed 14 August 2025 shall be dismissed together with the father’s Application in a Proceeding filed 9 October 2025.
Despite this being the case, and for all of the reasons discussed throughout these reasons, even if the court had been satisfied that there had been a change of circumstances, the court is not satisfied that there should be further litigation regarding the parenting arrangements for these children, as the same would not be in the best interests of these children.
In refusing to reopen the parenting proceedings, the court is cognisant that in his Outline of Case filed 2 February 2026 the father suggested that “before any coercive enforcement is ordered, the Court should obtain current, independent ascertainment of the children’s views in order to determine workability, risk, and appropriate next steps”.
The court however is of the view that such an arrangement would not be in these children’s best interests, for all of the reasons already discussed but in addition because:
(a) The children’s views, in all of the circumstances identified throughout these reasons and the trial reasons, are unlikely to be uninfluenced by the father;

(b) The children’s ages and level of maturity, when combined with all the findings made in the trial reasons regarding risk of harm, are not ones which warrant the children’s views being given substantial nor determinative weight; and

(c) The children have already been overexposed to a number of professionals post the making of the final orders, and it would not be in their best interests to expose them to further, non-therapeutic professional intervention.

ANCILLARY ORDERS

In circumstances where the court has determined that there is no basis for the court to revisit the parenting arrangements for the children, the only issues requiring further consideration is what parenting orders are necessary to effect the final orders.
The mother asks the court to make a recovery order together with a number of injunctions directed to the father to ensure that the children remain in her care.
During the hearing on 13 February 2025 the father consented to arrangements which it was hoped would avoid the need for any recovery order to be actioned, and the children removed from the father’s care by officers from the Australian Federal Police. Those arrangements include orders providing for:
(a) The children to be placed into childcare with Court Children’s Services immediately prior to the hearing at which these reasons are to be published and orders made, which were made on 23 February 2026 at the same time that the proceedings were listed for delivery of judgment;

(b) The father leaving the precincts of the court immediately after the conclusion of this hearing; and

(c) The father providing to the mother the children’s personal belongings.

Whilst a recovery order shall be made, the court is hopeful that the same will not be necessary because the children will be able to transition to the mother’s care immediately following the delivery of these reasons in the precincts of the court, with the father immediately removing himself from the court precinct.
I otherwise consider it appropriate to make injunctions as proposed by the mother to ensure that the father does not remove the children from her care and/or frustrate their transition to her primary care.

HARMFUL PROCEEDINGS

Legal Principles

The power of the court to make a harmful proceedings order is found in s 102QAC of the Act (which is found in Part XIB of the Act), which provides:
Making harmful proceedings orders
(1) A court exercising jurisdiction in proceedings under this Act may make an order (a harmful proceedings order) prohibiting a party (the first party) to the proceedings from instituting proceedings under this Act against another party to the proceedings without the leave of the court under section 102QAG, if the court is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe that:
(a) the other party would suffer harm if the first party instituted further proceedings against the other party; or

(b) in the case of child‑related proceedings (within the meaning of Part VII)—the child who is the subject of the proceedings would suffer harm if the first party instituted further proceedings against the other party.

Note: Proceedings includes cross‑proceedings and incidental proceedings (see subsection 4(1)).
(2) For the purposes of subsection (1), harm may include, but is not limited to, the following:
(a) psychological harm or oppression;

(b) major mental distress;

(c) a detrimental effect on the other party’s capacity to care for a child;

(d) financial harm.
(3) In determining whether to make an order under subsection (1), the court may have regard to:
(a) the history of the proceedings under this Act between the first party and the other party; and

(b) whether the first party has frequently instituted or conducted proceedings against the other party in any Australian court or tribunal (including proceedings instituted (or attempted to be instituted) or conducted, and orders made, before the commencement of this section); and

(c) the cumulative effect, or any potential cumulative effect, of any harm resulting from the proceedings referred to in paragraphs (a) and (b).

(4) The court may make a harmful proceedings order on its own initiative or on application by a party to the proceedings.

(5) The court must not make a harmful proceedings order in relation to a person without hearing the person or giving the person an opportunity of being heard.

(6) An order made under subsection (1) is a final order.

Order about notifying other party in relation to application for leave etc.
(7) If the court makes an order under subsection (1), the court must also make an order as to whether the court is to notify the other party, in the event that the first party makes an application under section 102QAE for leave to institute proceedings against the other party, of either or both of the following:
(a) that the application has been made;
(b) if the application is dismissed—that the application has been dismissed.
(8) The court must have regard to the wishes of the other party in making an order under subsection (7).
The decision whether or not to make a harmful proceedings order is discretionary, so long as the preconditions in s 102QAC(1) and (5) of the Act are met.
The power to make a harmful proceedings order was a new provision inserted in the Act when amendments came into force on 6 May 2024, as a result of the Family Law Amendment Act 2023 (Cth).
The Explanatory Memorandum to the Family Law Amendment Bill 2023 (Cth), on the topic of harmful proceedings orders, relevantly variously identified:
(a) In the General Outline:
The Bill will provide a new mechanism for the court to respond to and deter a known form of harassment, that being the repetitive filing of applications by one party to oppress another party. Often termed ‘systems abuse’, this tactic can be employed by perpetrators of family violence to manipulate the legal system to exert control over, threaten and harass a current or former partner. This can have substantial detrimental impacts on the physical and psychological health of the respondent, and on any children involved in proceedings. The Bill will provide the courts with a new power to scrutinise the impact of the institution of further proceedings to prevent systems abuse occurring.

(b) And specifically in relation to the relevant provisions:

Item 6 also inserts a new Division 1B [sic] which contains provisions relating to the court’s new power to make harmful proceedings orders. The purpose of this measure is to protect the respondent and/or children who are the subject of proceedings from the harmful impact of frequent and unnecessary applications filed by an applicant. This measure aims to limit systems abuse, which is a form of family violence that is prevalent in the family law system.

Discussion

As is clear from the orders made 30 January 2026, the court of its own volition raised the making of a harmful proceedings order directed to the father.
In the mother’s written submissions, she promoted the making of a harmful proceedings order directed to the father, submitting that there were “reasonable grounds” for the court to be satisfied that the “mother and the children have suffered psychological harm and major mental distress and there are reasonable grounds to find they would suffer harm if the current proceedings were continued or further proceedings were issued”.
Similarly in the written submissions of the ICL, the ICL promoted the making of a harmful proceedings order directed to the father, only if, the court determined that it would not reopen the parenting proceedings “without any carve out for appeal”.
Thus, in circumstances where the court has dismissed the father’s applications before the court, it is apparent that the ICL supports the making of the harmful proceedings order.
The court however does not accept the position promoted by the ICL that if a harmful proceedings order were to be made directed to the father, there should be a “carve out” for any appeal. This is because doing so defeats the very purpose and intention of a harmful proceedings order, to prevent further litigation, harm and systems abuse. Moreover, Part XIB, Division 1B of the Act prescribes the pathway for any application for leave to institute proceedings by the father together with the considerations for the court when determining any such application for leave.
Turning then to the question of whether a harmful proceedings order ought be made in all of the circumstances of these proceedings. The court holds significant concerns, as discussed throughout these reasons and the trial reasons, as to the father’s conduct, and any emotional and/or psychological harm the same has already caused to the children.
As earlier identified, findings were made by Jarret J in the trial reasons that the father has already exposed the children to emotional and psychological harm. Findings were also made that any psychological and emotional harm the children might suffer would be greater if they were to remain in the father’s care as against transitioning to the mother.
In light of all of the matters discussed throughout these reasons, the court is satisfied that:
(a) All of the father’s conduct since the making of the orders on 7 August 2025, has likely perpetuated and caused ongoing emotional and psychological harm to both children; and

(b) The father has acted with dogged, one eyed determination to undermine the jurisdiction of the court. That has included not only his conduct in the immediate aftermath of the making of the final orders, but also his conduct in the shadow of awaiting judgment following the conclusion of the trial, where he facilitated the reestablishment of the children’s relationship with the mother, only to snatch it away from them in what the court apprehends to have been an act of manipulation and coercion, and which completely ignored the impact of that conduct on the children and the emotional and psychological harm that conduct foreseeably might cause them.

It is for all of these reasons that the court is satisfied that if further parenting proceedings were to be instituted by the father, there are reasonable grounds to believe that those further proceedings would cause the children to suffer further and ongoing emotional and psychological harm.
It is also for all of these same reasons that the court is also satisfied that the father’s conduct is very likely to have had a deleterious impact upon the mother’s emotional and psychological functioning; particularly as she has the benefit of the orders and reasons of 7 August 2025, yet the father’s poor conduct and control of her relationship with the children appears to have continued unabated. It is for these reasons that the court apprehends that further litigation instituted by the father, particularly against the backdrop of the litigation which has occurred, is likely to have both a deleterious psychological impact on the mother but additionally make it harder for the mother to parent these children.
Neither of the parties addressed the court about the provisions raised in s 102QAC (7) and (8). Accordingly, no orders shall be made in that regard.
For all of these reasons, a harmful proceedings order shall be made directed to the father.
